context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my climate action course. I’m a little unsure how to organize my city’s info around the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation. Can you help me figure out how to start?
exchanges.tutor: Great that you're diving in! Before we figure out how to organize your info, let's start with what you already know. Which city have you chosen as your case study, and can you name any of the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course material?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and needs orientation without being given the answer. I should ask them what they already know to activate prior knowledge before providing any scaffolding. Keeping it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I picked Rotterdam. I’m not totally sure I remember all 5 pathways though—I think one is about infrastructure, but I might be mixing it up. Can you help me recall them first so I can sort my notes correctly?
exchanges.tutor: Rotterdam is an excellent choice — lots of climate action happening there! As for the pathways, rather than me listing them, your course materials are the best place to refresh your memory. Once you've looked them up, come back and share what you find — even a partial list works. What do you have access to right now in terms of course notes or readings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to recall the pathways for them, which would be giving them the answer directly. Instead, I should redirect them to their course materials while acknowledging their partial memory. Rotterdam is a great choice with lots of material available. I'll prompt them to look it up themselves.

exchanges.student: I have my course page open, but I was kinda hoping for a quick hint because I’m not sure what section to check. Is this the week on urban transformation frameworks, or should I be looking in the assignment page itself?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment page itself is a good starting point — it mentions the 5 Pathways directly. Give it a read and report back with what you find!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for navigation help within the course, which is a reasonable practical question. I can point them generally to the assignment page without giving away the content. This is a minor logistical nudge, not a substantive answer.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll check that. If I find the pathway names, can I paste them here and ask if I’m matching Rotterdam examples to the right ones?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely, that's exactly the right approach. Find the pathways, then share what Rotterdam examples you're thinking of connecting to them — we can work through the matching together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable and productive question about how to proceed. I should encourage this approach as it aligns well with guided discovery - they find the pathways, then work through matching their Rotterdam examples themselves. I'll confirm this is a good plan without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: Thanks — that helps. I’ll pull the pathway names first, then I want to test a few Rotterdam policies because I’m not confident which category they fit in.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds like a solid plan. Go ahead and come back when you're ready — I'm looking forward to seeing what you find!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear and productive plan. I should affirm it briefly and let them get to work without over-scaffolding or adding unnecessary commentary.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll do that first and then come back with my list.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — take your time and come back when you're ready!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stepping away to do independent research, which is exactly what we want. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Got it. I’ll be back once I’ve got the pathway names and a few Rotterdam examples.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeating the same message essentially. No new content to respond to. A very brief acknowledgment is sufficient.
The student is just signing off briefly. A short acknowledgment is all that's needed. No academic content to address.

exchanges.student: I’m ready now. I found the pathway names, and I want help checking whether my Rotterdam examples actually fit them.
exchanges.tutor: Great, let's see what you've got! Go ahead and share the pathway names and the Rotterdam examples you're thinking of connecting to them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is back and ready to work. I should prompt them to share both the pathway names they found and their Rotterdam examples so we can work through the matching together using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I have the names now, but I’m still a little unsure and don’t want to mislabel them from memory. Can you wait while I paste the exact list and my examples together so I don’t mix anything up?
exchanges.tutor: Of course, take your time and paste everything when you're ready!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being careful and methodical, which is good practice. A brief encouraging response is all that's needed here.
